- The contract documentation lacks specific details regarding the requirements for the peer review of the structural design of the precast box culvert. Therefore, I suggest that we initiate a discussion with the client to address this matter.
- In accordance with section A6.1 of the Waka Kotahi Highway Structures Design Guide, culverts with an opening area larger than 3.4m² fall under the category 3 structure. Table A1 of the Highway Structures Design Guide specifies that a Design Review Certificate is required for Category 3 structures. However, it's worth considering that the Pinnacle Sealed Box Culvert is a proprietary design with a successful track record in numerous other New Zealand projects. Given this, we may explore the possibility of exempting it from the requirement for a PS2 (Peer Review Certificate) for its use in the C2C3 project.

content:
_Captured Monday, 30 September 2019 12:32 a.m. · General › General / Misc_

![modification-to-ex](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/gen_025_modification-to-existi_1.png)

- Reference Documents;
- Building Act 2004
- The Building Act does not require existing buildings to achieve compliance with the current Building Code. Upgrading of existing buildings is not required unless they are considered dangerous or earthquake-prone (Building Act sections 121 and 124).
- However, new building work undertaken when the building is being altered needs to comply fully with the Building Code. The existing building should perform no worse than it did prior to the alterations taking place (Building Act section 112). The structural aspects of the existing building may therefore need to be upgraded if the building is undergoing a change of use (Building Act sections 114 and 115).
- From < http://www.seismicresilience.org.nz/topics/resilient-design/codified-seismic-design/building-upgrades/ >

title: Shear centre
content:
_Captured Thursday, 19 December 2024 10:45 am · General › Analysis & Fundamentals_

**Diagrams**
![shear-centre](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/gen_061_shear-centre_1.png)

- Reference documents: Design Guide 9- Torsional Analysis of Structural Steel Members
- The shear centre of an open cross section is the point where the resultant moment of external forces and internal stresses in the plane of the section must be zero.
- For symmetric shapes, the shear centre is on the line of symmetry.
- For doubly symmetric shapes, such as W-shapes, the shear centre is at the centroid.
- For channels, see Figure 3.1 and the associated equations for e0.
- For angles and tees, the shear centre is at the intersection of the midlines of the elements of the cross-section.

title: Design Risks
content:
_Captured Sunday, 24 March 2019 9:33 p.m. · General › Consents & Practice_

- Sample;
- TAN 0XX - BarrierDetailUpdate
- Deck Deflection;
- Estimating deflection is inherently difficult and there is a potential wide range of deflections that can occur outside the mean deflection based on our calibrated models.
- Risk
- Impact
- Mitigation
- Beams do not deflect as modelled (deflect more than expected)
- (Less likely)
- More levelling course required to fill to finish level due to more deflection in beams or;
- Raise barriers to allow for reduced hog deflection
- The designer has provided a upper bound of expected (mean) deflections. Raising barriers is not a high probability.
- Beams do not deflect as modelled (deflect less than expected)
- (Quite likely)
- Potential concrete cover issues to bridge deck, which mean the road approaches have to be raised to suit deck level.
- Survey beam deflection at each stage and compare to design value provided. Inform designer and adjust as required.
- Bridge clearance
- Clearance

title: Storm water Bridge
content:
_Captured Thursday, 4 July 2019 10:32 a.m. · General › Bridge Design_

- Design Criteria
- http://www.aucklanddesignmanual.co.nz/regulations/codes-of-practice/stormwatercodeofpractice/guidance/design/designcriteria
- Mladen
- Mark Groves
- Some else asked this the other day. Generally there is NZTA Specification P46 to cover SW design for new NZTA roads. However, bridge deck drainage also has its own nuances which isn't specifically covered in P46.
- Requirements about keeping flow out of the live lanes stands on a bridge same as anywhere else.
- We now generally try and treat the run-off from the bridge as well, rather than discharging over the side direct to the river. The latter is harder to consent and generally not something mana whenua are keen on either.
- Examples like the Taramakau or Ahaura Bridge, the AC is used to form a gutter between the AC pavement and the barrier. This is sized to convey a WQ flow (typically designed to convey 80% of the annual run-off volume) back to and beyond the abutment/s for treatment. Any excess flow is then spilt through the barrier via letter box openings which keeps down the scale of the gutter needed. The movement joint at the abutment then needs to be as waterproof as possible, but some leakage is accepted. This is the simplest way of

title: Shored construction
content:
_Captured Sunday, 1 January 2023 10:03 AM · General › General / Misc_

- Referenced documents;
- AASHTO LRFD Bridge Design Specifications-9th Ed (US)
- For shored construction, all permanent load shall be assumed applied after the concrete deck has hardened or has been made composite and the contract documents shall so indicate.
- While shored construction is permitted according to these provisions, its use is not recommended. There has been limited research on the effects of concrete creep on composite steel girders under large dead loads. Also, there have been only a very limited number of demonstration bridges built with shored construction in the U.S. Furthermore, there is an increased likelihood of significant tensile stresses occurring in the concrete deck at permanent interior supports of continuous spans when shored construction is used. These provisions may not be sufficient for shored construction where close tolerances on the girder cambers are important.

title: Sonic Test
content:
_Captured Wednesday, 25 February 2026 10:49 am · General › General / Misc_

**Diagrams**
![sonic-test](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/gen_227_sonic-test_1.png)

- Thank you for the summary. Just to be clear on terminology, I assume the following from your table below. There are a handful of different types of “pile integrity tests” so I’m avoiding that term to prevent confusion.
- “Sonic Test” = Cross-hole Sonic Logging (CSL)
- “Pile Integrity Test” = Sonic Pulse Echo (SPE)
- Sonic Pulse Echo testing is an inferior test method that is generally less reliable than CSL testing. That is one reason why CSL testing is in the spec. and why it is commonly used for concrete bridge piles. There are a number of factors in our particular case that may contribute to poor or useless results from SPE including large change in soil stiffness, presence and termination of casing near rock, aspect ratio of pile, and stiff rock at toe of pile. If a defect exists, it will be difficult or impossible to detect anything below it with this test. So if a defect exists near the top of the pile, we would actually get less information than if we did CSL testing for a partial length of the pile.
- “Therefore, the results produced from the PIT [SPE] test should not be regarded as the sole determining c

- All reinforcing prior to concrete pours or covering over (eg with formwork) including prefabricated elements
- Installation of precast beams
- Installation of precast elements
- All compressible filler board prior to covering over or pouring concrete
- Inspection of shear keys after galvanising but prior to installing denso tape
- All formwork prior to placement of reinforcing and/ or concrete
- Grouting of shear keys into precast beams
- All joints in deck prior to placing surfacing
- Hold Points
- The Constructor shall not proceed beyond a Hold Point until the Designer has expressly released the Hold Point in writing.

title: Issue with mix design
content:
_Captured Friday, 3 August 2018 10:43 a.m. · General › Materials & MSQA_

- DA879 ( QA5019HEB2)
- Query
- We note that the cement content is higher than allowed for in the project specifications. Please confirm with the fabricator that this mix design has been used previously for this application with no adverse effects in terms of workability, rate of strength gains, and subsequent dimensional change of units. If they haven’t used it before, it may need to be adjusted after the initial casting to achieve appropriate rate of strength development.
- Response
- The precast fabricator has confirmed this concrete mix
- QA5019HEB2 is the standard mix used daily in the Precast yard with no adverse effects on workability, strength gains, nor has there been any issues with shrinkage.
- The appropriate/designed transfer strengths with be achieved for all items cast using this mix.

title: Joint Waterproofing
content:
_Captured Thursday, 17 June 2021 3:52 PM · General › Bearings & Joints_

- Sika
- SikaSwell profiles can be used in combination with precast panels, some standard details are attached. The key thing is that SikaSwell must be encapsulated with concrete or grout. Minimum cover requirements are outlined on the Product Data Sheets. They work by swelling against concrete to form a gasket type seal when in contact with water. If concrete cover is insufficient the consequent pressure can crack concrete causing spalling.
- We see two potential issues with the detailed joint seal.
- Potential incompatibility with bitumen.
- Insufficient confinement – the SikaSwell will simply swell to displace and fill surrounding voids. It will be ineffective at sealing.
- Sikadur Combiflex SG system could be applied on the inside of the culvert as a joint sealing system. Cover plates would be needed to protect from traffic and support from potential hydrostatic pressure from behind (images below).
- Sika Emseal DSM-DS system mayalso be a good joint sealing solution in this case. Cover plates for traffic protection may be advisable – will confirm.
- Emseal is made to measure and sourced from USA.
- Lead times apply for manufacture and freight.
- Air freight is available but expensive.
- Factory made corn

- We have reviewed the mix design provided in NTE008 and following are our comments. In addition, as the question about concrete testing was raised in the meeting on 30 Oct 2018, the following provides some clarification of the requirement:
- Please provide mix design 402 and 402P for our information as required by Clause 2.10.3 of NZS 3104.
- In general, we are happy with the mix design 402T, however, please provide water content or W/C ratio for our information.
- It is not acceptable to use concrete mix 401T for the piles. As required by section 2.1.4.1 of “TC21/18 SH36 Pedestrian Overbridge – Structural Concrete Technical Specification” cast in-situ concrete shall have coarse aggregate particles of at least 19mm nominal size.

**Reference documents**
- T-CES-101-nz_NORMAL CONCRETE IN GENERAL
- TM 35 - NZ_Guide_to_Concrete_Construction
- Issue 498 - Preparation-for-concrete-floor-slabs_ofRFRnt
- Blinding and sulfate resistance
- BRE Special Digest 1, Section D6.5 Provide a sacrificial layer (APM4) notes that if the blinding is to be adopted as an Additional Protective Measure, the blinding concrete should be at least 50mm thick and of the same quality as the foundation construction. Therefore, if the blinding is being used as an APM it needs to be of the same quality as the foundation, but if this is not the case and the blinding is only used as a working platform, it does not have to be sulfate resistant.
- Similarly, BS 8500-1:2023 cl A.4.7 notes that ‘other than blinding or similar applications, standardized prescribed concretes (ST) should not be used where the presence of sulfates or other aggressive chemicals in the groundwater, the ground or any adjacent material give a ACEC classification higher than AC-1.´ Again this implies that blinding (ST or equivalent designated concrete) does not need to be sulfate resistant.

- The Whanawhana Bridge is a single-lane structure located on a non–state highway rural road with low traffic volumes and a low-speed environment. In consideration of these site conditions, Clause B3.1.4 of the Bridge Manual permits the use of a TL3 barrier system. In accordance with Clause B6.2, where a semi-rigid barrier system is installed on a single-lane bridge, the transverse design loads may be taken as 67 % of those specified in Table B4 of the Bridge Manual. Furthermore, Clause A1.4 allows the working width on single-lane bridges to be reduced to 270 mm for semi-rigid barriers. Accordingly, the use of a proprietary top mounted TL4 Thriebeam guardrail system, such as RamShield High Containment HC barrier installed along the bridge top face at sides is considered acceptable. Thriebeam barrier is considered acceptable provision for the occasional presence of people as per Bridge Manual cl B2.9.

title: Fatigue - Bridge - Construction load
content:
_Captured Sunday, 21 March 2021 11:47 PM · General › Loads & Actions_

- Reference documents;
- Kaituna river bridge Tauranga eastern link composite steel bridge design case study Edwards Rajasingh Lipscombe
- fatigue damage caused by the ADTs is cumulative to the number of cycles specified in the HERA document.
- The effect of fatigue due to the articulated dump trucks running across the bridge is calculated based on the number of trucks crossing per day over the period it will be used as a haul road. The number of fatigue cycles assumed is 548,000, which is a conservative estimate based on the number of trips across the bridge. To determine the effect of the ADT trucks on the bridge fatigue life, the following steps were taken;
- Divide the ‘number of actual cycles from the ADT truck’ with the ‘allowable number of cycles correlating to the stress in the element considered’.
- Divide the ‘number of stress cycles for the 100year design (standard fatigue loading)’ with the ‘number of allowable cycles at that stress range’.
- The sum of both fractions must be equal to or less than 1. This method was accepted by the external Peer Reviewer and Client.

Therefore a competitive design fee would be around 5% of this (structural only – note hydrology, geotechnical and planning add to this). Therefore a fee of around $100k for the structural design would be considered appropriate.
- Overall, including MSQA, geotech investigations, survey, planning I would expect a fee around 10-12% or 200-250k.

- Reference documents.
- PN 27 - Dairy Farm Infrastructure
- The clause A5.16.1 of PR, followed by NTT_FH_015, requires the stock underpass to be designed as per “IPENZ Practice Note 27” requirements.
- As per clause 5.8.1 of the PN 27 the Stormwater or groundwater that enters or collects in the underpass to be treated as FDE.
- The reviewer is on the idea that the designer to specify the method of the treatment (capture, transfer, storage and irrigation options, …) and to justify the acceptance of the proposed option.
- Designer to confirm the concept of 50% design stage of stuck underpass drawings match with the drawings and documents submitted to the Waitomo District Council for the consent exemption. And both the Principle agent and the WDD are happy with Treatment of stock effluent and runoff from the underpass, apron slabs and access tracks to be excluded from the project scope.

title: Concrete Repair - Surface Top Up
content:
_Captured Friday, 5 July 2019 9:08 p.m. · General › Concrete Design_

- Reference Documents;
- Overlay to Bridge Deck

**Methodology as follows**
- Creating Falls using Renderoc Paveroc
- Prepare the existing concrete surface by grinding to allow for adequate mechanical and chemical attachment of overlay
- Apply Nitobond EP (Epoxy Tie coat) to prepared surface and allow to go 'tack/
- Whilst Tacky, apply the Fosroc Renderoc Paveroc to the required area,
- Thicker sections will require multiple lifts with Renderoc Paveroc
- Min. 15mm thickness at thinner sections
- Screed Renderoc Paveroc to the desired falls
- Apply Concure A99 curing compound and allow to cure
- Avista Decorative Overlay

- Using a rubbing stone or similar, rub down any high spots or trowel marks in the Avista Resurfacing compound
- If stencil being used, apply the stencil to the trowelled Avista Resurfacing Compound and secure
- Spray/Hopper apply Avista Resurfacing Compound (colour of owner’s choice) to the area
- If flecks or colour affects are chosen repeat the process using dif

- Beam Deflection Formula
- Hi Kwan
- I can’t comment on the relationship between strength and elastic modulus, or the appropriate transfer time, but could the following material-related factors have contributed:
- Was the compressive strength measured on match-cured cylinders?
- Was the concrete shortening significantly as a result of ongoing cooling or drying after transfer?
- Were the deflections greater than those observed on previous castings from similar concrete? If so, has the concrete supplier changed materials, eg higher cement content (to achieve 35MPa as soon as possible in cold weather) or different coarse aggregate source/size/content, or different mix design?
- Was air content correct?
- Was the load applied slower than usual?
- NZS 3101 5.2.3(c) says to use f’c +10
- Perhaps the Bridge Manual is more informative?
- Also ask Dave Idle or Dejan, or one of the bridge team in Chch – someone will have encountered this before.
- Regards

- plastic shrinkage, chemical shrinkage, thermal shrinkage and drying shrinkage
- Plastic shrinkage
- wet concrete
- chemical
- thermal
- drying
- hardened concrete
- Chemical shrinkage; occurs rapidly in the days and weeks after casting and is less dependent on the environment and the size of the specimen than drying shrinkage
- Thermal shrinkage; is the contraction that results in the first few hours (or days) after setting.
- endogenous shrinkage
- Drying and autogenous shrinkage both increase with time at a decreasing rate.
- Drying shrinkage may continue for many years depending on the size and shape of the specimen, but autogenous shrinkage is essentially complete at about 50 days after setting.

- Fixings that are self-tapping or self-drilling without a plug may be suitable.
- Expandable fixings where a nylon plug is inserted into a hole and a pin driven or screwed into the plug can take slightly heavier loads.
- Other methods include flexible pins that are driven straight into the no-fines that deflect around the aggregate lodging in the void and expanding foam adhesives.

- ACI 360R-10 Guide to Design of Slabs-on-Ground
- Slabs-on-ground are considered non-structural elements where applied loads are passed directly though the slab to the ground.
- Cracking in non-structural slabs-on-ground can be mitigated and controlled to a significant extent by the proper use of contraction, construction, expansion, and isolation joints.
- Other approaches to mitigating or controlling cracks, such as expansive cement concrete, post-tensioning, or steel and synthetic fiber reinforcement, are not covered in this chapter, but are covered in ACI 360R.
- Other factors can influence cracking, such as subgrade support, friction between slab and subgrade, reinforcement quantity and details, concrete materials, mixture proportions, and curing and ambient weather during construction.
- Application time;
- It is desirable to install contraction joints early, before volume changes of the concrete occur due to contraction from cooling or shrinkage from moisture loss. Contraction joints should be in place be

- Has chemical analysis of the water been carried out to determine the softness of the water?
- This may affect the exposure class for the elements in contact with running water.
- Chemical analysis has not been undertaken.
- Table 3.1 of NZS 3101 assumes that members are in constant contact with water whereas Table 8 of NZS 3109 provides further information and differentiates between predominantly submerged and alternating wet and dry conditions. For alternating wet and dry conditions, NZS 3109 classifies the exposure as either B1 or B2 regardless of water hardness. Classification U is only applied to the flowing, predominantly submerged condition.
- The column bases are above the low water level and normal water levels fluctuate throughout the day based on outflows from the upstream dams. As such, the columns are considered to be in the alternating wet and dry condition and therefore exposure classification B2 is considered appropriate in accordance with NZS 3109.

title: Construction Joint
content:
_Captured Friday, 18 November 2022 9:01 AM · General › General / Misc_

- Reference documents;
- CA 02 - Suspended concrete floors - maximum size of pour allowable and location of construction joints
- Each construction joint is a potential weakness in the slab and liable to be the location of a slab opening.
- The maximum slab area to be poured at one time will be dependent upon the following factors
- The importance attached to the avoidance of cracking
- The position and size of vertical members (Columns, walls,…)
- The rigidity of restraints
- The likely degree of shrinkage and the percentage of reinforcement
- Thermal and moisture movements
- The use of high cement content concrete poured in hot weather is more likely to lead to early thermal cracking.
- Progressive (consecutive) sequences of pours are preferred to alternate pours
- if overall tensions are produced due to restrained contraction, will open to form straight crack lines.

title: Structural lightweight concrete
content:
_Captured Thursday, 19 February 2026 9:35 am · General › Concrete Design_

- ACI 213R-14 will tell you all you need to know about structural lightweight concrete properties and design. ACI 318 also defines characteristics of structural lightweight concrete. You can download ACI documents from Opusweb http://intranet/external/standards .
- ASTM STP 169D describes structural lightweight aggregate concrete as having an equilibrium density between 1440-1840kg/m3 and compressive strength >17MPa. Equilibrium density is density of hardened concrete after 90 days of air drying and is used for calculating design loads. For precast units or critical load conditions occur during construction, fresh density should be used to calculate self-load prior to equilibrium density being achieved (ACI 213R 5.3.2). ASTM STP 169D says that acceptance limits during construction should be based on fresh density.
- Thus you need to specify the required compressive strength (ultimate, and possibly at an earlier age depending on construction process), fresh density, and equilibrium density. Fresh density would be determined from trial mix/es of the proposed concrete that delivers the required strength and equilibrium densities.

title: SPT Test
content:
_Captured Thursday, 12 November 2020 12:44 PM · General › Geotechnical & Foundations_

**Diagrams**
![spt-test](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/gen_565_spt-test_1.png)

- Reference documents;
- Field-guide-sheet-description-of-soil-and-rock-2005-3
- Soils chart.250203
- How is the SPT performed?
- A split-barrel sampler is driven into the soil at the bottom of a borehole using a hammer of specific weight and fall height.
- The number of blows required to drive the sampler 300 mm into the soil is recorded as the SPT-N value.
- Parameters measured?
- Provides the SPT-N value, which is correlated with soil properties like density, strength, and compressibility.
- Accuracy and Reliability
- Less reliable for cohesionless soils (e.g., loose sands) and very soft clays.
- Application in Foundation Design
- Widely used in traditional foundation design for estimating bearing capacity and settlement,
- Test procedure
- Decide on
- the total depth
- Groundwater or hard strata (10m)
- intermediate test depth
- The Test shall be made at every change in the stratum or at intervals of not more than 1.5 meters whichever is less.

- Traditional soil classification systems (e.g., USCS) are based on laboratory-determined physical characteristics, such as grain size distribution and plasticity, which are measured on remoulded samples.
- The CPT measurements provide a repeatable index of the aggregate behaviour of the in-situ soil in the immediate area of the probe.
- The prediction of soil type based on CPT measurements is referred to as the Soil Behaviour Type (SBT).

- A cone penetrometer is pushed into the soil at a constant rate using a hydraulic system.
- It continuously measures the
- Cone resistance, resistance at the tip (cone resistance), q
- c
- = Q/A
- resistance along the shaft (sleeve

- Windows Search> Anaconda Prompt
- To check conda installed; Conda --version
- To see which environment you are in: Conda env list
- Create new environment: conda create --name
- Test
- Activate environment; conda activate
- Test
- Deactivate environment: conda deactivate
- Test

**Spyder**
- After activating the environment conda install spyder-kernels
- Run Anaconda navigator
- Lunch Spyder
- Change environment
- To install package: conda install jupyter-lab
- To install package from conda forge: conda install -c conda-forge condastats
- Lunching packages: Jupyter-lab
- Exist package: Ctrl+C

title: GitHub
content:
_Captured Friday, 22 May 2026 7:47 am · General › General / Misc_

- Reference Documents: Models and pricing for GitHub Copilot - GitHub Docs
- WSP GitHub Enterprise
- WSP GitHub Enterprise Managed User (EMU)
- Water Design and Draughting Efficiency Initiative
- Hi team, General
- This is the introduction and first update regarding a process underway to transition water design & draughting to a nationally consistent delivery model across the Water Sector, Regions (Water), and the Water Global Capability Centres (GCC).
- Our work will focus on how we consistently work
- efficiently
- together to produce
- high quality and consistent outputs
- .
- It is not a change in team structures etc. - this is about the supporting systems and processes that will allow anyone, anywhere to
- work seamlessly across clients and WSP
- .
- Our initial focus is pipeline design & draughting in Civil 3D (but this will expand over time).

**What is happening now and in the coming weeks**
- Digital leads from Water and Transport are in the process of adopting a single CAD standard from one of the many in use around the business - this should be finalised soon.
- Digital leads from Water and Transport are considering systems and usage, and who should maintain them and potential use of the Global CAD Depl
